content.text: Hard negatives are training examples that are semantically similar to an anchor text but are not its true positive — the model finds them difficult to distinguish, producing strong gradient signal and forcing it to learn fine-grained semantic distinctions. They are the single most impactful factor in contrastive embedding training quality after data scale: a model trained on 10,000 pairs with hard negatives consistently outperforms one trained on 100,000 pairs with random negatives.
What Are Hard Negatives

In contrastive learning, a negative is any text that should be far from an anchor in the embedding space. Negatives exist on a difficulty spectrum determined by their cosine similarity to the anchor. A random negative (e.g. 'Python is a language' paired with an anchor about aspirin) is easily separated — the embedding model already knows these topics are unrelated, and the loss for such a triplet is near zero.

A hard negative, by contrast, is a passage that is topically adjacent or uses similar vocabulary to the anchor, but answers a different question or describes a different entity. For a query 'What is the mechanism of aspirin?', the passage 'Ibuprofen also inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, reducing prostaglandin synthesis' is a hard negative: it uses the same drug-mechanism vocabulary, the same domain, and even mentions the same enzymes — but it is about a different drug.

The difficulty is precisely why hard negatives are valuable: they force the model to learn representations that distinguish not just broad topics but fine-grained differences within a topic — the kind of distinctions a useful retrieval system must make.

Hard negatives are typically defined operationally as passages that appear in the top-k nearest neighbours of an anchor according to an ANN index (high vector similarity), but are not the true positive for that anchor. A margin parameter separates hard negatives from potential false negatives — passages too close to the true positive may themselves be relevant and must be excluded.
Why Hard Negatives Matter

attrs.alt: Gradient signal comparison: easy negatives produce near-zero gradient while hard negatives force the model to learn fine-grained distinctions

content.text: The gradient produced by a contrastive loss is proportional to the degree to which the loss constraint is violated. For a triplet (anchor, positive, negative) with Triplet Loss:

If d(a, n-) is already much larger than d(a, p+) + m, the loss is zero and the gradient is identically zero. The model receives no update. This happens constantly with random negatives after the first few training epochs — the model correctly identifies that they are unrelated without effort, but makes no progress on distinguishing similar-sounding texts.

For InfoNCE loss, the same principle applies through the softmax: if the negative's similarity is very low, the softmax denominator is dominated by the positive, and the gradient signal from that negative is negligible. Only negatives with similarity scores close to the positive — the hard negatives — contribute meaningfully to the gradient.

This is the 'easy negative collapse' failure mode: a model trained only with random in-batch negatives quickly achieves near-perfect performance on those negatives (they are easily separated) but never learns to distinguish hard cases. On domain-specific retrieval benchmarks, such models underperform models trained with hard negatives by 10–20 percentage points in recall@10.
Hard negatives provide non-zero gradient throughout training, preventing the model from plateauing early.
They force the model to encode subtle semantic differences, producing embeddings with higher resolution for fine-grained retrieval.
Domain-specific hard negatives are especially powerful: they expose the model to the actual confusion patterns that arise in production (e.g. aspirin vs. ibuprofen, not aspirin vs. tennis rackets).
Hard negative quality correlates directly with final retrieval recall@k — this is consistently the highest-leverage improvement in contrastive model training.
Benefits
Dramatically higher retrieval quality on in-domain benchmarks: improvements of 10–25% in recall@10 vs. random negatives are common in domain-specific settings.
More efficient training: a model reaches a given quality level in fewer gradient steps when training with hard negatives, because each step provides more useful signal.
Produces sharper embedding clusters: the embedding space becomes more discriminative within semantic neighbourhoods, enabling precise similarity thresholds for deduplication and clustering tasks.

Self-improving curriculum: as the model improves, ANN-based hard negatives become progressively harder (the model gets better at separating what used to be hard), creating a natural training curriculum.
Generalisation: training on hard negatives requires the model to look beyond surface-form similarity, producing representations that generalise better to unseen domain text.
Enables fine-grained deduplication: with hard-negative-trained embeddings, near-duplicate detection can use lower cosine thresholds with fewer false positives.
Trade-offs
Mining cost: identifying hard negatives requires encoding the full corpus and running ANN search for each query — an expensive operation that must be repeated at every epoch for dynamic mining.

False negative risk: semantically relevant passages that appear as hard negatives will corrupt the gradient by teaching the model to push relevant content away from the anchor. This is the most dangerous failure mode.
Training instability: very hard negatives (similarity nearly equal to the positive) can cause gradient explosions, especially at low InfoNCE temperatures. A margin filter mitigates this.

Circularity: the quality of mined hard negatives depends on the current model — a poor initial model produces low-quality hard negatives, which limits early-stage training. A curriculum (easy first, then hard) addresses this.
Infrastructure complexity: dynamic hard negative mining requires maintaining an updatable vector index during training, adding engineering overhead compared to simple in-batch negatives.
Label noise propagation: if the original (query, positive) labels are noisy, hard negatives mined from a noisy corpus will amplify the noise.
Types of Hard Negatives

attrs.alt: Hard negative mining pipeline: from corpus through encoding, FAISS indexing, ANN search, and filtering to produce training triplets

content.text: BM25-Based Hard Negatives

BM25 retrieves passages that share keywords with the anchor query. These are hard negatives from a lexical perspective — they match query terms but may not be semantically relevant. They are the cheapest hard negatives to mine (no embedding model required), and useful in the early stages of fine-tuning before the model is strong enough to produce useful ANN-based negatives.

BM25 hard negatives are particularly effective for tasks where the model must distinguish between texts that share terminology but differ in meaning — a common failure mode for embedding models that focus too much on token overlap. Example: for query 'aspirin mechanism of action', BM25 retrieves 'aspirin is used for fever' (different aspect) and 'ibuprofen mechanism of action' (different drug, same aspect).

from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
import re

def tokenise(text: str) -> list[str]:
    """Simple lowercase word tokeniser. Use spaCy or NLTK for production."""
    return re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9 ]", " ", text.lower()).split()


def mine_bm25_negatives(
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
    n_negatives: int = 5,
) -> list[tuple[str, str, str]]:
    """
    Mine hard negatives using BM25 retrieval.
    Passages that score highly against the query but are not the positive
    are good lexical hard negatives.
    """
    tokenised = [tokenise(doc) for doc in corpus]
    bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenised)

    triplets = []
    for qi, (query, pos_idx) in enumerate(zip(queries, positive_indices)):
        q_tokens = tokenise(query)
        scores = bm25.get_scores(q_tokens)  # float array, len == len(corpus)

        # Rank by BM25 score descending, skip true positive
        ranked = sorted(
            [(score, idx) for idx, score in enumerate(scores) if idx != pos_idx],
            reverse=True,
        )
        negs_found = 0
        for score, cand_idx in ranked:
            if score <= 0:
                break  # BM25=0 means no keyword overlap — not a hard negative
            triplets.append((query, corpus[pos_idx], corpus[cand_idx]))
            negs_found += 1
            if negs_found >= n_negatives:
                break

    return triplets


# Example
queries = [
    "mechanism of action of aspirin",
    "warfarin drug interactions",
]
corpus = [
    "Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes.",       # q0 positive
    "Aspirin is widely used for pain relief and fever reduction.",   # BM25 hard neg for q0
    "Ibuprofen mechanism of action: reversible COX inhibition.",    # BM25 hard neg for q0
    "Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1).",      # q1 positive
    "Warfarin requires regular INR monitoring every 2-4 weeks.",    # BM25 hard neg for q1
    "Python is a high-level programming language.",                  # unrelated
]
positive_indices = [0, 3]

bm25_triplets = mine_bm25_negatives(queries, corpus, positive_indices, n_negatives=2)
for anchor, pos, neg in bm25_triplets:
    print(f"Q:  {anchor}")
    print(f"P+: {pos[:60]}")
    print(f"N-: {neg[:60]}")
    print()
# Q:  mechanism of action of aspirin
# P+: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 enzymes.
# N-: Ibuprofen mechanism of action: reversible COX inhibition.
#
# Q:  warfarin drug interactions
# P+: Warfarin inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1).
# N-: Warfarin requires regular INR monitoring every 2-4 weeks.
ANN-Based Hard Negatives (Semantic Mining)

ANN-based mining uses the embedding model itself to identify hard negatives. The corpus is encoded, indexed in FAISS, and for each query, the top-k nearest neighbours are retrieved. Non-positive neighbours with similarity below a margin threshold are the hard negatives. These are semantically harder than BM25 negatives because they are similar at the embedding level, not just the keyword level.

ANN-based negatives are typically mined iteratively: train for 1–2 epochs with BM25 or in-batch negatives, then re-encode the corpus with the updated model, mine new hard negatives, and continue training. Each iteration produces harder negatives because the model has learned to separate previously-easy pairs.

import numpy as np
import faiss
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from typing import Optional


class HardNegativeMiner:
    """
    Mines hard negatives from a corpus using ANN search.
    Supports iterative re-mining as the model improves.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        model: SentenceTransformer,
        margin: float = 0.05,        # neg must score <= pos_score - margin
        n_candidates: int = 200,     # ANN candidates per query
        n_negatives_per_query: int = 5,
        use_gpu: bool = False,
        index_type: str = "flat",    # 'flat' for exact, 'hnsw' for approximate
    ):
        self.model = model
        self.margin = margin
        self.n_candidates = n_candidates
        self.n_neg = n_negatives_per_query
        self.use_gpu = use_gpu
        self.index_type = index_type
        self._index: Optional[faiss.Index] = None
        self._corpus_vecs: Optional[np.ndarray] = None

    def build_index(self, corpus: list[str], batch_size: int = 256) -> None:
        """Encode corpus and build FAISS index. Call after each model update."""
        print(f"Encoding {len(corpus)} corpus documents...")
        self._corpus_vecs = self.model.encode(
            corpus,
            normalize_embeddings=True,
            batch_size=batch_size,
            show_progress_bar=True,
        ).astype(np.float32)
        dim = self._corpus_vecs.shape[1]

        if self.index_type == "flat":
            self._index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(dim)
        elif self.index_type == "hnsw":
            # HNSW is faster for large corpora but approximate
            self._index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(dim, 32)
            self._index.hnsw.efSearch = 64
        else:
            raise ValueError(f"Unknown index_type: {self.index_type}")

        if self.use_gpu:
            res = faiss.StandardGpuResources()
            self._index = faiss.index_cpu_to_gpu(res, 0, self._index)

        self._index.add(self._corpus_vecs)
        print(f"Index built: {self._index.ntotal} vectors, dim={dim}")

    def mine(
        self,
        queries: list[str],
        corpus: list[str],
        positive_indices: list[int],
    ) -> list[dict]:
        """
        Mine hard negatives for a set of (query, positive_index) pairs.
        Returns list of dicts with anchor, positive, negatives.
        """
        if self._index is None:
            self.build_index(corpus)

        q_vecs = self.model.encode(
            queries, normalize_embeddings=True, batch_size=256
        ).astype(np.float32)

        scores_all, indices_all = self._index.search(q_vecs, self.n_candidates)

        results = []
        skipped_false_neg = 0
        for qi, (q_scores, q_indices) in enumerate(zip(scores_all, indices_all)):
            pos_idx   = positive_indices[qi]
            pos_score = float(self._corpus_vecs[pos_idx] @ q_vecs[qi])

            negatives = []
            for cand_idx, cand_score in zip(q_indices, q_scores):
                if cand_idx == pos_idx:
                    continue
                if cand_score > pos_score - self.margin:
                    # Too close to positive — likely a false negative
                    skipped_false_neg += 1
                    continue
                negatives.append({
                    "text": corpus[cand_idx],
                    "score": float(cand_score),
                    "pos_score": pos_score,
                })
                if len(negatives) >= self.n_neg:
                    break

            results.append({
                "anchor":    queries[qi],
                "positive":  corpus[pos_idx],
                "negatives": negatives,
            })

        print(f"Mined hard negatives for {len(queries)} queries; "
              f"skipped {skipped_false_neg} potential false negatives.")
        return results


# Usage
model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
miner = HardNegativeMiner(model, margin=0.05, n_candidates=100, n_negatives_per_query=3)

queries = ["What is the mechanism of aspirin?",
           "How does metformin lower blood sugar?"]
corpus = [
    "Aspirin irreversibly acetylates and inhibits COX-1 and COX-2.",         # pos for q0
    "Aspirin is a first-line analgesic for mild to moderate pain.",           # BM25 near, hard neg
    "Ibuprofen inhibits COX enzymes reversibly, unlike aspirin.",             # hard neg q0
    "Metformin activates AMPK and suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis.",       # pos for q1
    "Metformin is excreted renally and contraindicated in severe CKD.",       # hard neg q1
    "SGLT-2 inhibitors lower glucose by increasing urinary glucose excretion.", # semi-hard neg q1
]
positive_indices = [0, 3]

miner.build_index(corpus)
results = miner.mine(queries, corpus, positive_indices)
for r in results:
    print(f"Anchor: {r['anchor']}")
    print(f"  P+: {r['positive'][:70]}")
    for n in r["negatives"]:
        print(f"  N- (sim={n['score']:.3f}): {n['text'][:70]}")
    print()
Cross-Encoder-Scored Mining (Gold Denoising)

Cross-encoder denoising is the highest-quality but most expensive hard negative mining strategy. After ANN retrieval produces candidate hard negatives, each candidate is scored by a cross-encoder (a model that takes (query, candidate) jointly as input and outputs a relevance score). Candidates with cross-encoder scores above a threshold are likely actual false negatives and are excluded from training.

This strategy was used to train models like E5-large-v2 and BGE-large, where cross-encoder denoising of web-scale crawled data produced clean (query, positive) and (query, hard_negative) pairs with very low false-negative rates. The cross-encoder acts as a quality filter and annotator.

from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder, SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np
import faiss


def mine_with_ce_denoising(
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
    bi_encoder: SentenceTransformer,
    cross_encoder: CrossEncoder,
    n_ann_candidates: int = 50,
    ce_relevance_threshold: float = 0.5,  # CE score above this → likely false negative
    n_negatives: int = 3,
) -> list[tuple[str, str, str]]:
    """
    Two-stage hard negative mining:
    Stage 1: Bi-encoder ANN retrieves top-k candidates.
    Stage 2: Cross-encoder filters out likely false negatives.
    Returns (anchor, positive, hard_negative) triplets.
    """
    # Stage 1: ANN retrieval
    corp_vecs = bi_encoder.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    q_vecs    = bi_encoder.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)

    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corp_vecs.shape[1])
    index.add(corp_vecs)
    scores_all, indices_all = index.search(q_vecs, n_ann_candidates)

    triplets = []
    for qi, (q_scores, q_indices) in enumerate(zip(scores_all, indices_all)):
        pos_idx = positive_indices[qi]
        # Filter out true positive
        candidates = [
            (corpus[ci], float(cs))
            for ci, cs in zip(q_indices, q_scores)
            if ci != pos_idx
        ][:n_ann_candidates]

        if not candidates:
            continue

        # Stage 2: Cross-encoder scoring of candidates
        ce_pairs = [(queries[qi], cand_text) for cand_text, _ in candidates]
        ce_scores = cross_encoder.predict(ce_pairs)  # relevance probabilities

        # Keep only candidates that the cross-encoder considers NOT relevant
        # (i.e. confirmed negatives, not false negatives)
        confirmed_negs = [
            cand_text
            for (cand_text, _), ce_score in zip(candidates, ce_scores)
            if ce_score < ce_relevance_threshold
        ]

        for neg_text in confirmed_negs[:n_negatives]:
            triplets.append((queries[qi], corpus[pos_idx], neg_text))

    print(f"Cross-encoder denoised triplets: {len(triplets)}")
    return triplets


# Example: use a lightweight cross-encoder for quality filtering
bi_encoder    = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

queries  = ["mechanism of aspirin"]
corpus   = [
    "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 irreversibly.",          # true positive
    "Aspirin is used for pain, fever, and anti-platelet therapy.", # likely false neg
    "Ibuprofen is a reversible COX inhibitor, unlike aspirin.", # good hard negative
    "COX enzymes produce prostaglandins from arachidonic acid.", # semi-relevant
    "Python is a programming language created by Guido.",       # easy negative
]
positive_indices = [0]

triplets = mine_with_ce_denoising(
    queries, corpus, positive_indices,
    bi_encoder, cross_encoder,
    n_ann_candidates=4, ce_relevance_threshold=0.5, n_negatives=2,
)
for a, p, n in triplets:
    print(f"A: {a}")
    print(f"P: {p[:70]}")
    print(f"N: {n[:70]}")
Dynamic and Iterative Hard Negative Mining

Static hard negative mining runs once before training: encode corpus, mine negatives, freeze the triplet dataset, train to convergence. Dynamic mining updates the negatives periodically during training — typically every epoch or every K steps. As the model improves, its current embedding space defines what is 'hard', producing progressively more challenging negatives.
Dynamic mining is the standard approach in state-of-the-art models like E5 (Wang et al., 2022), BGE (Xiao et al., 2023), and GTE. The training loop alternates between:
Encode corpus with current model weights (expensive but necessary for fresh negatives).
Build FAISS index from fresh corpus vectors.
Mine hard negatives for each query using the updated index.
Train for one epoch on the refreshed triplet dataset.
Repeat from step 1.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
import numpy as np
import faiss


def mine_negatives_from_current_model(
    model: SentenceTransformer,
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
    margin: float = 0.05,
    n_neg: int = 3,
) -> list[InputExample]:
    """Mine hard negatives using the current model weights. Called every epoch."""
    corp_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True,
                             batch_size=256, show_progress_bar=False).astype(np.float32)
    q_vecs    = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True,
                             batch_size=256, show_progress_bar=False).astype(np.float32)

    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corp_vecs.shape[1])
    index.add(corp_vecs)
    scores_all, indices_all = index.search(q_vecs, 50)

    examples = []
    for qi, (q_scores, q_indices) in enumerate(zip(scores_all, indices_all)):
        pos_idx   = positive_indices[qi]
        pos_score = float(corp_vecs[pos_idx] @ q_vecs[qi])
        negs = []
        for ci, cs in zip(q_indices, q_scores):
            if ci == pos_idx or cs > pos_score - margin:
                continue
            negs.append(corpus[ci])
            if len(negs) >= n_neg:
                break
        # Create (query, positive, hard_neg_1, hard_neg_2, ...) examples
        # MNRL treats elements 2+ as additional hard negatives
        texts = [queries[qi], corpus[pos_idx]] + negs
        examples.append(InputExample(texts=texts))
    return examples


def iterative_contrastive_training(
    base_model_name: str,
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
    n_outer_epochs: int = 5,
    n_inner_epochs: int = 1,
    batch_size: int = 32,
) -> SentenceTransformer:
    """
    Outer loop: re-mine hard negatives each epoch.
    Inner loop: train on current hard negatives.
    """
    model = SentenceTransformer(base_model_name)
    loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model=model)

    for outer in range(n_outer_epochs):
        print(f"\n=== Outer epoch {outer + 1}/{n_outer_epochs}: mining hard negatives ===")

        # Step 1: Mine hard negatives with current model
        examples = mine_negatives_from_current_model(
            model, queries, corpus, positive_indices
        )
        print(f"  Mined {len(examples)} training examples")

        # Step 2: Train on new examples
        loader = DataLoader(examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=batch_size)
        model.fit(
            train_objectives=[(loader, loss_fn)],
            epochs=n_inner_epochs,
            warmup_steps=max(1, len(loader) // 10),
            show_progress_bar=False,
        )
        print(f"  Inner training complete (loss_fn={loss_fn.__class__.__name__})")

    return model


# Usage
queries = ["What does aspirin do?", "How is insulin resistance measured?"]
corpus = [
    "Aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes.",
    "Aspirin reduces fever and inflammation.",         # false negative candidate
    "Ibuprofen also inhibits COX enzymes reversibly.", # hard neg for q0
    "HOMA-IR measures insulin resistance via fasting glucose and insulin.",
    "Insulin resistance is associated with type 2 diabetes.",  # false neg candidate
    "HbA1c measures 2-3 month average blood glucose.",        # hard neg for q1
    "Python is a programming language.",                       # easy neg
]
positive_indices = [0, 3]

trained_model = iterative_contrastive_training(
    "BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5",
    queries, corpus, positive_indices,
    n_outer_epochs=3, n_inner_epochs=2, batch_size=2,
)
print("Iterative training complete.")

A common production schedule is three outer epochs: epoch 1 uses BM25 negatives (no embedding needed), epoch 2 uses ANN negatives from the BM25-trained model, and epoch 3 uses ANN negatives from the epoch-2 model. Each stage provides harder negatives than the previous one, implementing a natural curriculum.
The False Negative Problem

False negatives are the most dangerous failure mode in hard negative mining. A false negative is a corpus passage that IS semantically relevant to the anchor query but is treated as a negative during training. When the model receives a gradient signal pushing it to make the anchor and this relevant passage dissimilar, it learns the wrong thing — the exact opposite of what training should teach.

False negatives arise naturally in any dense retrieval corpus: for a query about 'aspirin mechanism', multiple passages in a biomedical corpus may describe different aspects of aspirin's action (analgesic, anti-platelet, anti-inflammatory). Any of them retrieved by ANN search could be a false negative if they are not the designated positive.
Three strategies mitigate false negatives:

Margin filtering: exclude any ANN candidate whose similarity to the anchor is within a margin of the true positive's similarity. The intuition is that passages very close to the positive in embedding space are likely also relevant. A margin of 0.05–0.1 filters most false negatives with minimal loss of hard negatives.
Cross-encoder denoising: score each candidate with a cross-encoder and exclude those scoring above a relevance threshold. This is expensive but highly accurate.

Corpus deduplication before mining: if the corpus contains near-duplicates of the positive (which is common in web-scraped data), deduplication removes most false negative candidates before they can be mined.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, CrossEncoder
import numpy as np
import faiss


def detect_false_negatives(
    query: str,
    positive: str,
    candidates: list[str],
    cross_encoder: CrossEncoder,
    relevance_threshold: float = 0.5,
) -> tuple[list[str], list[str]]:
    """
    Split candidates into confirmed negatives and potential false negatives.
    Returns (confirmed_negatives, potential_false_negatives).
    """
    pairs = [(query, c) for c in candidates]
    ce_scores = cross_encoder.predict(pairs)  # relevance probabilities [0, 1]

    confirmed = []
    false_negs = []
    for cand, ce_score in zip(candidates, ce_scores):
        if ce_score >= relevance_threshold:
            false_negs.append((cand, float(ce_score)))
        else:
            confirmed.append(cand)
    return confirmed, false_negs


def mine_with_false_neg_filtering(
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
    model: SentenceTransformer,
    cross_encoder: CrossEncoder,
    n_neg: int = 5,
    margin: float = 0.05,
) -> dict[str, list[str]]:
    """
    Combined: ANN mining + margin filter + cross-encoder denoising.
    Reports how many candidates were removed as false negatives.
    """
    corp_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
    q_vecs    = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)

    index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corp_vecs.shape[1])
    index.add(corp_vecs)
    _, idx_all = index.search(q_vecs, 30)

    report = {"total_candidates": 0, "margin_filtered": 0,
              "ce_filtered": 0, "confirmed_negatives": 0}
    all_triplets = []

    for qi, q_indices in enumerate(idx_all):
        pos_idx   = positive_indices[qi]
        pos_score = float(corp_vecs[pos_idx] @ q_vecs[qi])

        # Stage 1: margin filter
        after_margin = []
        for ci in q_indices:
            if ci == pos_idx:
                continue
            report["total_candidates"] += 1
            sim = float(corp_vecs[ci] @ q_vecs[qi])
            if sim > pos_score - margin:
                report["margin_filtered"] += 1
                continue
            after_margin.append(corpus[ci])

        # Stage 2: cross-encoder denoising
        confirmed, false_negs = detect_false_negatives(
            queries[qi], corpus[pos_idx], after_margin[:20],
            cross_encoder, relevance_threshold=0.5,
        )
        report["ce_filtered"] += len(false_negs)
        report["confirmed_negatives"] += len(confirmed[:n_neg])

        for neg in confirmed[:n_neg]:
            all_triplets.append((queries[qi], corpus[pos_idx], neg))

    print(f"Mining report: {report}")
    return {"triplets": all_triplets, "report": report}


# Example
model        = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
cross_encoder = CrossEncoder("cross-encoder/ms-marco-MiniLM-L-6-v2")

q = ["What are the side effects of metformin?"]
c = [
    "Metformin causes GI side effects including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea.", # pos
    "Metformin is contraindicated in severe renal impairment.",   # false neg (still relevant)
    "Metformin lowers blood sugar by reducing hepatic glucose output.", # false neg
    "Ibuprofen side effects include GI irritation and renal impairment.", # hard neg
    "Side effects of warfarin include bleeding and skin necrosis.",  # hard neg
    "Python 3 was released in 2008.",  # easy neg
]
result = mine_with_false_neg_filtering(q, c, [0], model, cross_encoder, n_neg=2)
print(f"Confirmed triplets: {len(result['triplets'])}")
Curriculum Learning with Hard Negatives

Curriculum learning applies negatives in order of increasing difficulty, matching the training stage to the model's current capability. An untrained model cannot benefit from very hard negatives — it cannot distinguish them from positives and the gradients are too noisy. A well-trained model gets little benefit from random negatives — the constraint is already satisfied.
A practical curriculum has three phases:
Phase 1 (epochs 1–2): Random in-batch negatives. The model learns basic topical separation — medical text vs. code vs. news. Large batch size (128+) provides sufficient diversity.
Phase 2 (epochs 3–5): BM25 hard negatives. The model learns to separate texts that share keywords but differ in meaning — same drug, different aspects; same domain, different entities.
Phase 3 (epochs 6–10): ANN hard negatives with margin filtering. The model learns fine-grained semantic distinctions — same drug, same aspect, different mechanism details.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
import re, numpy as np, faiss


def curriculum_contrastive_training(
    model_name: str,
    queries: list[str],
    corpus: list[str],
    positive_indices: list[int],
) -> SentenceTransformer:
    model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)

    # ── Phase 1: Random in-batch negatives (epochs 1-2) ──────────────────────
    print("Phase 1: Random in-batch negatives")
    phase1_examples = [
        InputExample(texts=[queries[i], corpus[positive_indices[i]]])
        for i in range(len(queries))
    ]
    loader1  = DataLoader(phase1_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=32)
    loss_fn  = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(model)
    model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader1, loss_fn)], epochs=2, show_progress_bar=False)

    # ── Phase 2: BM25 hard negatives (epochs 3-5) ────────────────────────────
    print("Phase 2: BM25 hard negatives")
    tokenised_corpus = [re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9 ]", " ", doc.lower()).split() for doc in corpus]
    bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenised_corpus)
    phase2_examples = []
    for i, query in enumerate(queries):
        pos_idx = positive_indices[i]
        scores  = bm25.get_scores(re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9 ]", " ", query.lower()).split())
        candidates = [ci for ci in np.argsort(-scores) if ci != pos_idx][:5]
        texts = [query, corpus[pos_idx]] + [corpus[ci] for ci in candidates[:3]]
        phase2_examples.append(InputExample(texts=texts))
    loader2 = DataLoader(phase2_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=16)
    model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader2, loss_fn)], epochs=3, show_progress_bar=False)

    # ── Phase 3: ANN hard negatives from updated model (epochs 6-10) ─────────
    print("Phase 3: ANN hard negatives (iterative, 2 outer epochs)")
    for outer in range(2):
        corp_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
        q_vecs    = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
        index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corp_vecs.shape[1])
        index.add(corp_vecs)
        _, idx_all = index.search(q_vecs, 30)
        phase3_examples = []
        for i, (q_idx_list) in enumerate(idx_all):
            pos_idx   = positive_indices[i]
            pos_score = float(corp_vecs[pos_idx] @ q_vecs[i])
            negs = [corpus[ci] for ci in q_idx_list
                    if ci != pos_idx and
                    float(corp_vecs[ci] @ q_vecs[i]) < pos_score - 0.05][:3]
            phase3_examples.append(InputExample(texts=[queries[i], corpus[pos_idx]] + negs))
        loader3 = DataLoader(phase3_examples, shuffle=True, batch_size=16)
        model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader3, loss_fn)], epochs=2, show_progress_bar=False)
        print(f"  Outer epoch {outer + 1} complete")

    model.save("./curriculum-trained-model")
    return model


# Example usage
queries = ["Mechanism of aspirin?", "How to lower blood sugar?"]
corpus  = [
    "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 irreversibly.",
    "Aspirin is a salicylate analgesic.",             # BM25 hard neg
    "Ibuprofen inhibits COX reversibly.",             # ANN hard neg
    "Metformin suppresses hepatic gluconeogenesis.",
    "Metformin is first-line for type 2 diabetes.",   # BM25 hard neg
    "SGLT-2 inhibitors reduce glucose reabsorption.", # ANN hard neg
]
pos_idx = [0, 3]
trained = curriculum_contrastive_training("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5", queries, corpus, pos_idx)
When to Use Hard Negatives

Any domain-specific fine-tuning: if you are fine-tuning a general model on specialised content (medical, legal, scientific, code), hard negatives are essential because the general model was never exposed to within-domain confusion patterns.
After the first 1-2 epochs of in-batch negative training, when the easy negatives are all correctly separated and the model has stopped improving.
When your evaluation recall@k has plateaued and you need to push past a quality ceiling.
For datasets with many near-duplicates or topically similar documents (e.g. a corpus of drug monographs, legal clauses, or API documentation).
When you have access to a cross-encoder or labelled relevance data that can denoise the mined negatives — this enables the highest-quality hard negatives with minimal false negative risk.

Hard negatives are NOT needed if your retrieval task is coarse-grained (e.g. classifying between 10 very different topic areas) or if a general embedding model already achieves recall@10 > 0.85 on your domain evaluation set. In those cases, in-batch negatives suffice and mining complexity is not justified.
Full Implementation: Production Hard Negative Pipeline

"""
Production hard negative mining pipeline with:
- BM25 warm-start phase
- ANN-based mining with margin filter
- Optional cross-encoder denoising
- Metrics tracking
"""
import json
import numpy as np
import faiss
from pathlib import Path
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer, InputExample, losses
from sentence_transformers.evaluation import InformationRetrievalEvaluator
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
import re


class ProductionHardNegativePipeline:
    def __init__(
        self,
        base_model: str = "BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5",
        output_dir: str = "./hard-neg-model",
        margin: float = 0.05,
        n_ann_candidates: int = 200,
        n_negatives_per_query: int = 5,
        batch_size: int = 32,
    ):
        self.model = SentenceTransformer(base_model)
        self.output_dir = Path(output_dir)
        self.output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
        self.margin = margin
        self.n_cand = n_ann_candidates
        self.n_neg  = n_negatives_per_query
        self.batch_size = batch_size
        self.loss_fn = losses.MultipleNegativesRankingLoss(self.model)

    def _tokenise(self, text: str) -> list[str]:
        return re.sub(r"[^a-z0-9 ]", " ", text.lower()).split()

    def bm25_phase(self, queries, corpus, pos_idx, epochs=2):
        bm25 = BM25Okapi([self._tokenise(d) for d in corpus])
        examples = []
        for i, q in enumerate(queries):
            scores = bm25.get_scores(self._tokenise(q))
            neg_indices = [ci for ci in np.argsort(-scores) if ci != pos_idx[i]]
            texts = [q, corpus[pos_idx[i]]] + [corpus[ci] for ci in neg_indices[:self.n_neg]]
            examples.append(InputExample(texts=texts))
        loader = DataLoader(examples, batch_size=self.batch_size, shuffle=True)
        self.model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader, self.loss_fn)],
                       epochs=epochs, show_progress_bar=True)
        print(f"BM25 phase ({epochs} epochs) complete")

    def ann_phase(self, queries, corpus, pos_idx, n_outer=3, n_inner=2):
        for outer in range(n_outer):
            # Re-encode corpus with updated model
            corp_vecs = self.model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True,
                                          batch_size=256).astype(np.float32)
            q_vecs    = self.model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
            index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(corp_vecs.shape[1])
            index.add(corp_vecs)
            _, idx_all = index.search(q_vecs, self.n_cand)

            examples = []
            fn_skipped = 0
            for i, q_idx_list in enumerate(idx_all):
                pi = pos_idx[i]
                pos_score = float(corp_vecs[pi] @ q_vecs[i])
                negs = []
                for ci in q_idx_list:
                    if ci == pi:
                        continue
                    if float(corp_vecs[ci] @ q_vecs[i]) > pos_score - self.margin:
                        fn_skipped += 1
                        continue
                    negs.append(corpus[ci])
                    if len(negs) >= self.n_neg:
                        break
                examples.append(InputExample(texts=[queries[i], corpus[pi]] + negs))
            print(f"ANN outer {outer+1}: {len(examples)} examples, {fn_skipped} potential false-negs skipped")
            loader = DataLoader(examples, batch_size=self.batch_size, shuffle=True)
            self.model.fit(train_objectives=[(loader, self.loss_fn)],
                           epochs=n_inner, show_progress_bar=True)

    def train_and_save(self, queries, corpus, pos_idx, eval_queries=None, eval_corpus=None, eval_rel=None):
        print("=== Phase 1: BM25 warm-start ===")
        self.bm25_phase(queries, corpus, pos_idx, epochs=2)
        print("\n=== Phase 2: ANN hard negatives (iterative) ===")
        self.ann_phase(queries, corpus, pos_idx, n_outer=3, n_inner=2)
        self.model.save(str(self.output_dir))
        print(f"\nModel saved to {self.output_dir}")
        return self.model


# Minimal demo
pipeline = ProductionHardNegativePipeline("BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5")
print("Pipeline initialized. Call pipeline.train_and_save(queries, corpus, pos_idx) to train.")
Evaluating the Impact of Hard Negatives

The standard evaluation for hard negative mining is a held-out retrieval benchmark with Recall@k and NDCG@10. You want to compare the base model, the in-batch-negative-trained model, and the hard-negative-trained model on the same benchmark.

from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
import numpy as np


def eval_retrieval(model_name: str, queries: list[str], corpus: list[str],
                   relevant_ids: list[list[int]], k: int = 10) -> dict:
    model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
    q_vecs = model.encode(queries, normalize_embeddings=True)
    c_vecs = model.encode(corpus, normalize_embeddings=True)
    sim    = q_vecs @ c_vecs.T                    # (Q, C)
    ranked = np.argsort(-sim, axis=1)[:, :k]       # top-k per query

    # Recall@k
    recall = np.mean([
        len(set(ranked[i]) & set(rel)) / len(rel)
        for i, rel in enumerate(relevant_ids) if rel
    ])

    # NDCG@k
    def dcg(hits):
        return sum(1 / np.log2(r + 2) for r, h in enumerate(hits) if h)

    ndcgs = []
    for i, rel in enumerate(relevant_ids):
        if not rel:
            continue
        hits = [1 if r in rel else 0 for r in ranked[i]]
        ideal_hits = [1] * min(len(rel), k) + [0] * max(0, k - len(rel))
        ideal_dcg = dcg(ideal_hits)
        ndcgs.append(dcg(hits) / ideal_dcg if ideal_dcg > 0 else 0.0)

    return {f"Recall@{k}": round(recall, 4), f"NDCG@{k}": round(np.mean(ndcgs), 4)}


# Benchmark: biomedical retrieval mini set
queries = ["mechanism of aspirin", "metformin side effects", "warfarin monitoring"]
corpus = [
    "Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 irreversibly via acetylation.",  # rel: q0
    "Metformin causes nausea and diarrhoea; lactic acidosis is rare.", # rel: q1
    "INR monitoring is required for warfarin to target 2.0-3.0.",     # rel: q2
    "Ibuprofen reversibly inhibits COX-1 and COX-2.",                  # near-miss q0
    "Metformin is first-line for type 2 diabetes mellitus.",           # near-miss q1
    "Warfarin interacts with many drugs via CYP2C9.",                  # near-miss q2
    "Python is a general-purpose programming language.",               # unrelated
]
relevant = [[0], [1], [2]]

print(f"{'Model':<30} {'Recall@5':>10} {'NDCG@5':>10}")
print("-" * 50)
for name in ["BAAI/bge-base-en-v1.5"]:  # compare multiple checkpoints in practice
    m = eval_retrieval(name, queries, corpus, relevant, k=5)
    print(f"{name:<30} {m['Recall@5']:>10.4f} {m['NDCG@5']:>10.4f}")
# In production with fine-tuned model, typical improvements:
# Base:        Recall@5=0.667, NDCG@5=0.721
# + BM25 negs: Recall@5=0.778, NDCG@5=0.831
# + ANN negs:  Recall@5=0.889, NDCG@5=0.912
Common Mistakes

Mining negatives without a margin filter: passages retrieved as the top-1 ANN result for a query are frequently the true positive or a near-duplicate. Without a margin filter, these become false negatives that destroy training quality.

Using the same model for mining and training without updating: if you mine negatives with the initial model and never update the index, the negatives become progressively easier as training proceeds — exactly the easy-negative collapse problem you are trying to avoid.

Mining from a contaminated corpus: if the corpus contains the training pairs themselves (a common data leakage scenario), the model will mine the positive as a near-duplicate of itself, creating a false negative identical to the positive.

Setting the margin too large: a margin of 0.3–0.5 excludes too many valid hard negatives, leaving only very easy negatives. Start at 0.05 and increase only if you observe high rates of actual false negatives confirmed by a cross-encoder.

Ignoring batch composition during training with hard negatives: if all pairs in a batch are from the same narrow subdomain, in-batch negatives within that batch are hard negatives for every pair — creating many effective false negatives. Shuffle pairs across subdomains.

Not deduplicating the positive pool before mining: if two queries share an identical positive passage, their hard negatives may include each other's positives. Deduplicate positive passages at training data preparation time.
Evaluating with the same metric as the training loss: training loss decreasing does not guarantee recall@k improvement. Always maintain a separate held-out retrieval eval set.
Best Practices

Combine BM25 and ANN negatives: BM25 provides lexical hard negatives (good early), ANN provides semantic hard negatives (good later). A curriculum that starts with BM25 and graduates to ANN consistently outperforms either alone.
Use a margin of 0.05–0.10 for the false negative guard in ANN mining. If you have a cross-encoder, use it to validate your margin setting on a sample of 100–200 mined negatives.

Mine more candidates than you use (n_candidates >> n_negatives): retrieve 100–200 candidates and keep the 3–5 hardest that pass the margin filter. This gives you flexibility to filter without running out of negatives.

Track the false negative rate: periodically run a cross-encoder over a sample of mined negatives and measure what fraction are actually relevant (cross-encoder score > 0.5). A false negative rate > 20% signals your margin is too small or your corpus has too many relevant passages per query.

For iterative mining, update the FAISS index every epoch on small corpora (<100k docs) and every 2–5 epochs on large corpora (>1M docs). GPU-based FAISS (faiss-gpu) can encode 1M docs in under 5 minutes on a V100.

Store mined negatives to disk and cache them: re-mining from scratch is expensive. Save the triplet dataset after each mining phase and only re-mine if the model changes significantly (metric improvement > 0.05).
Consider curriculum order: random → BM25 → ANN (static) → ANN (dynamic). Each stage should be validated against the retrieval benchmark before advancing.
Performance and Scalability
Hard negative mining has two expensive operations: corpus encoding and ANN search. At scale (1M–100M documents), both require careful engineering.
Corpus Size

Index Type

ScaNN / HNSW distributed
For corpora under 1M documents, IndexFlatIP (exact cosine) is recommended: it guarantees no missed negatives and runs in 1–10 seconds.
For 1M–50M documents, IndexHNSWFlat with M=32 and efSearch=64 achieves 99%+ recall at 10–30x speedup over flat search.
For 50M+ documents, IndexIVFPQ (IVF with product quantisation) reduces memory from ~3 GB per 1M dims to ~500 MB per 1M dims, enabling single-machine indexing of 100M+ vectors.
faiss-gpu accelerates both encoding (via GPU batch encoding) and search. A V100 GPU handles roughly 1M similarity computations per millisecond.
Distributed mining across multiple machines: encode corpus shards independently, merge into a central index for search, distribute search queries across workers.

Compressing large to small models

For most domain fine-tuning tasks, the BM25 → ANN static → ANN dynamic curriculum provides the best quality-to-cost ratio. Cross-encoder denoising is reserved for scenarios where data quality is paramount and compute budget allows — for example, when training a model that will be deployed in a high-stakes clinical decision support system.
Security and Reliability

Hard negative mining exposes the model to semantically confusable texts from the corpus. If the corpus contains adversarially crafted texts (e.g. passages designed to be semantically close to many queries), these will be mined as hard negatives and systematically degrade retrieval quality. Sanitise the corpus before mining.

Iterative mining creates a feedback loop: if the model makes an error (mapping irrelevant text close to a query), it mines that text as a hard negative, reinforcing the error. Monitor for metric degradation between outer epochs and add an early-stopping criterion.

Corpus poisoning attacks: in open-corpus retrieval systems where users can contribute documents, an attacker can inject documents designed to be mined as hard negatives for specific queries, degrading retrieval quality for targeted queries. Implement contributor rate-limiting and semantic similarity deduplication of new corpus additions.

When using cross-encoder denoising, the cross-encoder becomes a security-critical component: if the cross-encoder itself has biases or attack surfaces, those propagate into the training data. Use validated, well-tested cross-encoder checkpoints.
Mental Model

Think of an embedding model as a cartographer drawing a semantic map. Initially, the map has a rough layout — medicine is far from sports, code is far from cooking. But within the medicine region, everything is clustered together indiscriminately: aspirin and ibuprofen are adjacent, warfarin and heparin are adjacent, even though clinically they are very different.

Hard negatives are like a cartographer's magnifying glass applied to confused regions of the map. They zoom into the medicine cluster and force the map to have finer resolution: aspirin (irreversible COX inhibitor) must be separated from ibuprofen (reversible COX inhibitor), even though they look similar at a coarse scale.

BM25 hard negatives find things that are in the same neighbourhood by reading keywords on the map labels — they look alike on paper. ANN hard negatives find things that are close in the current map — they look alike in the current embedding space. As the map gets refined, ANN negatives get harder, because the model must distinguish progressively more subtle differences.

The false negative filter is the cartographer double-checking: 'wait, this thing I was about to label as different — is it actually different, or did I just not recognise the connection?' The margin filter is a quick sanity check; the cross-encoder is the careful second review.
Key Takeaways
Hard negatives are semantically similar but non-relevant passages — their high similarity to the anchor makes them the most informative training signal in contrastive learning.

Random in-batch negatives saturate quickly: after a few epochs, the model easily separates unrelated texts and the gradient signal drops to near-zero. Hard negatives maintain a non-zero gradient throughout training.
Use a three-stage curriculum: BM25 negatives first (cheap, lexical), then ANN static negatives, then ANN dynamic negatives (re-mined from the current model each epoch).
The margin filter is essential: exclude any mined candidate whose similarity to the anchor exceeds (positive_score - margin). This prevents the most obvious false negatives from entering training.

Cross-encoder denoising provides the highest-quality negatives by confirming that each mined candidate is truly irrelevant before including it in training. Use it when data quality is critical and compute allows.
False negatives are the main failure mode: treating relevant passages as negatives teaches the model the wrong thing. Monitor false negative rate with a cross-encoder spot-check.

FAISS IndexFlatIP (exact cosine via inner product on normalised vectors) is the recommended index for hard negative mining — its exactness guarantees no negatives are missed due to approximation error.

Improvements from hard negatives over random negatives: typically 10–25% recall@10 increase on domain-specific retrieval benchmarks. This is the single highest-ROI improvement in embedding model fine-tuning.
